name: Magni
description: Magni is a god in Norse mythology, son of the thunder god Thor and the jötunn Járnsaxa. His name translates to "strong" or "powerful," reflecting his immense strength, which is his defining characteristic. He is primarily known for his role in lifting the leg of the giant Hrungnir off of his father Thor after Thor defeated the giant in a duel. Thor, impressed by his son's strength, gifted Magni the horse Gullfaxi, which had belonged to Hrungnir. While not as widely documented as other Norse deities, Magni represents the potential of strength and the passing of power from one generation to the next. Some accounts suggest he and his brother Móði will inherit Thor's hammer Mjolnir and survive Ragnarok to help rebuild the world, signifying their importance in the future of the Norse cosmos. His role, though relatively minor in existing texts, is suggestive of a future where strength and resilience are paramount. He symbolizes the enduring power within the lineage of the gods and the ongoing cycle of destruction and rebirth. He embodies raw, untamed power, a vital force in the ever-changing landscape of Norse cosmology. Magni's existence highlights the importance of progeny and the continuation of godly strength through generations, ensuring the survival and prosperity of the Aesir.
subtitle: God of Strength and Power

keyMyths.title: Lifting Hrungnir's Leg
keyMyths.description: Magni, at only three years old, was able to lift the leg of the giant Hrungnir off of Thor after Thor defeated him. This feat of strength impressed Thor greatly.

keyMyths.title: Inheritance of Gullfaxi
keyMyths.description: As a reward for his strength, Thor gifted Magni the horse Gullfaxi, which had previously belonged to the giant Hrungnir, showing the transfer of power and possession.

keyMyths.title: Survival of Ragnarok
keyMyths.description: Some accounts suggest that Magni and his brother Móði will survive Ragnarok and inherit Thor's hammer Mjolnir, playing a key role in rebuilding the world after the cataclysmic event.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
